answer:
**The work is the *Slokāntara* (author unknown), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This chapter illustrates the doctrine that "flaws and virtues arise from association" (Sanskrit śloka 45) through the story of two parrots: **Gāvākṣa**, raised by a scholar (in a hermitage), and **Girikā**, raised by a hunter. A king, separated from his retinue while hunting, arrives first at the hunter's house, where Girikā cries out:

> "Come on, come on! Smash his head!"[^1]

Frightened, the king flees to the scholar's hermitage, where Gāvākṣa welcomes him with refined hospitality:

> "Oh, fortunate are you, O King! My lord has deigned to visit the hermitage. Pray, rest, sir. Sit upon the new mat. Chew this betel nut and fresh betel leaf, with uncooked lime. If my lord is weary, pray, bathe, Your Majesty, in the spring."[^1]

The scholar explains that the parrots' contrasting behaviors stem from their associations (saṁsarga), just as virtues or vices in humans arise from the company one keeps.[^1]

[^1]: *Slokāntara*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-5797f7).
